title: Curry on Polyester
timeEstimate: 30–90 minutes; oil-strip mandatory + sunlight finish; honest cap on dyed polyester
stainChemistry: Curry is the toughest yellow-dye stain in common use because it has every category at once: turmeric pigment (the bright yellow that grips fibers fast and gets WORSE with alkaline cleaners), oil base (ghee, coconut oil, vegetable oil), other spice pigments (paprika, chili), and onion / garlic. On polyester, two complications stack: polyester is OLEOPHILIC and grabs the oil + dye combination tight, AND polyester is industrially dyed with disperse dyes at high heat — so any heat at home drives the turmeric pigment into the fiber the same way intentional dyeing does. The recurring traps: (1) hot water (sets the pigment), (2) chlorine bleach (turns turmeric pigment from yellow to RED-BROWN — worse than the original), and (3) alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide all darken turmeric). Honest cap: full removal on dyed polyester is often impossible — manage expectations.
whyThisWorks: Dawn strips the oil carrier that's holding the turmeric pigment in place. Once the oil is gone, 3% peroxide on white polyester partially oxidizes the yellow color. Sunlight is the second finisher — UV photodegrades turmeric pigment slowly over hours-to-days. The full sequence (Dawn oil-strip → cool peroxide → sunlight) is the most reliable home approach. Skip the oil-strip and peroxide can't reach the pigment. Use chlorine bleach and the yellow turns red-brown permanently. Use alkaline cleaners and the yellow darkens permanently. Order and bounded chemistry matter more on curry than on almost any other stain.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): curry is dominated by curcumin (the turmeric pigment) — a diketo-enol compound with a stable conjugated chromophore that turns red-brown under alkaline conditions and is photodegradable under UV. Pro uses POG / petroleum ether for oil + NSD + 3% H₂O₂ at controlled dilution + sunlight assist + acetic acid neutralizing rinse. Sodium hydrosulfite (reducing bleach) is the pro nuclear option for set yellow on white polyester.
defaultAssumption: Indian / Thai / Asian dinner spill on a polyester shirt or blend. Fresh = full sequence has a real shot. Hot-dried = often permanent yellow shadow. Dyed polyester = honest cap is partial removal; pro is the right call for valuable garments.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Plastic spoon edge or dull butter knife
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape solid bulk off with a plastic spoon edge. Flat-edge work only — never rub. Rubbing pushes pigment into the polyester surface texture.

spottingProtocol.technique: Strip oil carrier — mandatory before peroxide
spottingProtocol.instruction: Generous Dawn directly on the spot. Soft toothbrush 10 min. Polyester is oleophilic — extended dwell needed. CRITICAL — peroxide can't reach the pigment until the oil is stripped. Cool damp-cloth rinse.

spottingProtocol.agent: Repeat Dawn If Oily Feel Remains
spottingProtocol.instruction: Touch with a clean fingertip. Oily feel = repeat Dawn. Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes for curry oil.
Inside seam: dab 3% peroxide, blot with white cloth. If color transfers, skip peroxide. White polyester usually passes; dyed performance polyester often doesn't.
WHITE polyester only or hidden-test-passed: 3% peroxide damp cloth 5–10 min. NEVER mix with chlorine bleach (turns yellow to red-brown), ammonia, or alkaline cleaners (darken turmeric).

Hang the damp garment in direct sunlight for several hours. UV breaks down turmeric pigment slowly. Multi-day exposure for stubborn yellow. The most reliable finisher for curry — patience over hours-to-days, not aggressive chemistry.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, cool water, drying rack
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry. Faint yellow remaining = repeat sunlight, not chemistry. NEVER hot dryer until fully cleared — locks pigment into polyester permanently.

steps: 1. Scrape solid bulk gently — no rubbing.
2. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
3. Repeat Dawn pass — polyester needs 2 typically.
4. Hidden-area peroxide test on dyed polyester.
5. 3% H₂O₂ damp cloth for yellow on hidden-test-passed polyester.
6. Sunlight finisher — UV photodegrades turmeric over hours-to-days.
7. Cool wash + AIR-DRY. Honest cap on dyed polyester.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets turmeric pigment into polyester permanently
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach — turns turmeric yellow to RED-BROWN (worse than original)
NEVER alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide) — darken turmeric permanently
NEVER hot water — sets the pigment into polyester
NEVER hot dryer until fully cleared
Oil-strip BEFORE peroxide — Dawn first, peroxide second
Polyester is oleophilic — 2 Dawn passes typical
Honest cap: dyed polyester often plateaus at partial yellow shadow

homeSolutions.agent: Curry on Polyester Has an Honest Cap
homeSolutions.instruction: Curry is the toughest yellow-dye stain in common use because it's turmeric pigment + oil + heat-locking on polyester all at once. Full removal on dyed polyester is often impossible — set realistic expectations from the start. Three hard rules: NO chlorine bleach (turns the yellow RED-BROWN — worse than original), NO alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide all darken turmeric permanently), and NO hot water / hot dryer (locks the pigment into polyester forever). The path: oil-strip → peroxide on white only → sunlight for the rest.
Scrape solid curry off with a plastic spoon edge. Flat-edge work only. NEVER rub — rubbing pushes turmeric pigment into the polyester's surface texture and locks it in.

Apply a generous amount of Dawn dish soap directly to the spot. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 10 minutes. Polyester is oleophilic — it grabs the curry oil tight, so extended dwell is needed. This step is MANDATORY before peroxide. Without it, oil coats the pigment and peroxide can't reach it. Cool damp-cloth rinse.

homeSolutions.agent: Test for Oily Feel — Repeat If Needed
homeSolutions.instruction: Touch the spot with a clean fingertip. Still feels oily? Repeat the Dawn step. Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes for curry oil.

Before peroxide on dyed polyester: dab 3% peroxide on an inside seam, blot with a white cloth. If color transfers, skip peroxide entirely. White polyester usually passes; dyed athletic / performance polyester often doesn't.

homeSolutions.agent: 3% Peroxide for the Yellow — White Only
homeSolutions.instruction: WHITE polyester only or hidden-test-passed: 3% drugstore peroxide on a damp cloth, press 5 to 10 minutes, blot. Partially oxidizes the yellow.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: NEVER mix peroxide with chlorine bleach (toxic AND turns turmeric to red-brown), ammonia, or alkaline cleaners (toxic AND darken turmeric).

homeSolutions.agent: Sunlight Is the Real Finisher — Patience Wins
homeSolutions.instruction: Hang the damp garment in direct sunlight for several hours. UV breaks down turmeric pigment slowly — this is the most reliable finisher for curry. Multi-day exposure is normal for stubborn yellow. Sun + patience beats aggressive chemistry on this stain. If you don't have sun, a sunny window over multiple days works too.

Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry — faint yellow remaining? Repeat the sunlight, not more chemistry. NEVER hot dryer until cleared. Honest cap on dyed polyester: a faint yellow shadow may remain regardless. Pro is the next step for valuable garments.

steps: 1. Set honest expectations — dyed polyester often plateaus at partial yellow shadow.
2. NO chlorine bleach. NO alkaline cleaners. NO hot water / hot dryer.
3. Scrape solids gently with plastic edge.
4. Dawn pretreat cool 10 min — MANDATORY before peroxide.
5. Repeat Dawn pass if oily feel remains (2 passes typical).
6. Hidden-area peroxide test on dyed polyester.
7. 3% peroxide damp cloth for yellow on white / hidden-test-passed polyester.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop on hot-dried curry (often permanent yellow). Stop if hidden-test fails on dyed polyester. Stop if chlorine bleach was already used (turned yellow to red-brown — irreversible). Stop on performance / specialty polyester. Stop after full sequence + multi-day sunlight plateau — accept the honest cap.
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach — RED-BROWN reaction worse than original yellow
NEVER alkaline cleaners — darken turmeric permanently
NEVER hot water or hot dryer — locks pigment into polyester
Polyester is oleophilic — 2 Dawn passes typical
Sunlight is the finisher — patience over hours-to-days
Honest cap on dyed polyester — partial removal is the norm

neverDo: Never chlorine bleach on curry — turns yellow to red-brown permanently
Never alkaline cleaners on turmeric — darken permanently
Never hot water or hot dryer on curry + polyester
Never skip the Dawn oil-strip before peroxide
Never mix peroxide + chlorine + ammonia + OxiClean
fiberSensitivities: Polyester is oleophilic — grabs curry oil tightly, needs 2 Dawn passes
Polyester locks dye under heat — same chemistry as industrial dyeing
Performance / specialty polyester (compression, technical): pro — coatings react unpredictably
Dyed polyester: hidden test mandatory before peroxide; honest cap is partial removal
Turmeric is the polyester-and-cotton-specific lock pigment — UV photodegradation is the safest finisher

materialWarnings: Curry on polyester has an HONEST CAP — full removal is often impossible on dyed
Chlorine bleach is the most common irreversible failure — turns yellow to red-brown
Sunlight is the finisher — patience over hours-to-days
Polyester loves curry oil — Dawn × 2 is mandatory before peroxide

escalation.when: Hot water or hot dryer already used (often permanent). Chlorine bleach already used (turned yellow to red-brown — irreversible). Alkaline cleaner already used (darkened yellow permanently). Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester. Full sequence + multi-day sunlight plateau on dyed polyester (often the honest cap).
escalation.whatToTell: "Curry on [white / dyed / athletic / specialty] polyester, age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [scrape + Dawn × 2 + 3% peroxide on white + sunlight finisher × N hours/days + cool wash + air-dry]. No chlorine, no alkaline, no hot. [Residual yellow / red-brown reaction occurred / hot-dried suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with reducing-bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) chemistry — note even pro chemistry plateaus on hot-dried curry on dyed polyester

contextQuestions: Restaurant curry (more oil) or home-cooked (less oil)?
White, dyed, athletic, or specialty polyester?
Fresh, hours old, or hot-dried already?
Has chlorine bleach been used (often produces red-brown lock)?
Has alkaline cleaner (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone) been used?
Sunny location available for sunlight finisher?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Curry's turmeric pigment (turmeric pigment) is chemically the same lock as mustard and pure turmeric — same irreversibility under chlorine, same alkaline-darkening, same UV photodegradation. Polyester adds the oleophilic + heat-lock complications. Restaurant curry is harder than home-cooked because of more oil. Sunlight is genuinely the most reliable finisher — patience over multiple days beats aggressive chemistry. Honest cap is partial removal on dyed polyester; pro chemistry doesn't reliably do better.
